sections.heading: The national ECDI 2030 rose slightly
sections.body: The national Early Childhood Development Index 2030 increased from 76.8 percent in 2022 to 78.3 percent in 2025. This 1.5-point rise shows some improvement, but not a large national change. Because regional and socioeconomic results vary much more widely, the national figure should be treated as a broad summary, not as a picture of conditions everywhere in the country.

sections.heading: Regional results differed sharply
sections.body: Regional results in 2025 varied widely. Negros Island Region had the highest ECDI 2030 value at 93.0 percent, followed closely by Cagayan Valley at 92.2 percent. BARMM was much lower at 47.2 percent. The gap between the highest and lowest regional values was 45.8 points, which means the national level of 78.3 percent hides very different regional situations.

sections.heading: Some regions made large gains
sections.body: Some regions improved strongly between 2022 and 2025. Bicol Region rose from 73.2 to 84.4 percent, SOCCSKSARGEN from 71.9 to 81.7 percent, Cagayan Valley from 83.2 to 92.2 percent, and the National Capital Region from 73.9 to 81.0 percent. These increases show that the national rise was not spread evenly; it was driven in part by big gains in selected regions.

sections.heading: Some regions declined even as the national figure rose
sections.body: Several regions moved against the national trend. Zamboanga Peninsula¹ fell from 81.1 percent in 2022 to 67.3 percent in 2025, Eastern Visayas from 85.0 to 77.2 percent, Central Luzon from 84.8 to 78.9 percent, and Ilocos Region from 85.2 to 79.8 percent. These declines show that the national average can rise while individual regions still experience setbacks.

sections.heading: Most regions were near the national level, but BARMM was much lower
sections.body: In 2025, many regions were grouped around the high 70s and low 80s: CALABARZON at 78.4 percent, Central Luzon at 78.9 percent, Central Visayas at 79.5 percent, Davao Region at 79.6 percent, Ilocos Region at 79.8 percent, and CAR at 80.6 percent. BARMM, at 47.2 percent, was far below that group. This means the regional pattern is not just a gradual spread; one especially low regional value stands out from the more moderate differences among many regions.

sections.heading: The oldest age group was still lowest
sections.body: By age in months, children aged 48 to 59 months had the lowest ECDI 2030 value in 2025 at 75.1 percent, even though this was higher than 69.4 percent in 2022. Children aged 24 to 35 months were higher at 81.9 percent, while children aged 36 to 47 months were at 77.8 percent. The oldest group improved, but the age gap did not disappear.

sections.heading: Girls scored higher than boys
sections.body: Female children had an ECDI 2030 value of 83.3 percent in 2025, compared with 73.9 percent for male children. The gap was 9.4 points. It was wider than in 2022, when the values were 79.6 percent for female children and 74.3 percent for male children. This is one of the clearer demographic differences in the data.

sections.heading: Urban children remained ahead of rural children
sections.body: The residence gap continued in 2025. Urban children were at 80.4 percent, while rural children were at 76.0 percent. Both groups improved from 2022, when urban children were at 78.1 percent and rural children were at 75.4 percent, but the urban advantage widened from 2.7 to 4.4 points. This gap is smaller than the regional spread, but it still shows a consistent difference by residence.

sections.heading: Children who attended early childhood education had higher ECDI
sections.body: Children who attended early childhood education had an ECDI 2030 value of 83.4 percent in 2025, compared with 73.3 percent for children who never attended. The never-attended group stayed at 73.3 percent from 2022 to 2025, while the attended group rose from 80.1 to 83.4 percent. This does not prove that attendance alone caused the difference, but the link is clear in the data.

sections.heading: Children's ECDI was much higher when mothers had more schooling
sections.body: ECDI 2030 values generally increased as mothers' education rose. In 2025, children whose mothers had no education were at 59.1 percent, and children whose mothers had Grades 1 to 6 were at 61.4 percent. By comparison, the value was 84.6 percent for college and 88.2 percent for Grades 11 to 12. The gap between Grades 11 to 12 and no education was 29.1 points, making mothers' education one of the strongest divides in the page data.

sections.heading: Wealth still made a clear difference
sections.body: The lowest wealth quintile reached 70.9 percent in 2025, up from 66.2 percent in 2022. The highest quintile reached 85.0 percent, up from 83.5 percent. In 2025, the gap between the highest and lowest quintiles was 14.1 points. The second and fourth quintiles also improved, but the middle quintile declined from 80.3 to 75.4 percent, so the wealth pattern was broad but not perfectly linear.

takeaways: The national ECDI 2030 value rose from 76.8 percent in 2022 to 78.3 percent in 2025.
Regional differences were much larger than the national change, ranging from 47.2 percent in BARMM to 93.0 percent in Negros Island Region.
In 2025, ECDI values were higher for children who attended early childhood education, girls, urban children, and children of more educated mothers.
Wealth and mothers' education remained important divides. The lowest values were concentrated among children in the poorest households and among children whose mothers had little or no schooling.
footnotes: ¹ Includes the province of Sulu in 2025.
